director.summary: Week 17 (ending 29 April 2026) shows severe distortion from Easter timing shift and post-holiday demand collapse. Palmerstown down 18.2% total sales, Balbriggan down 15.0%, but Skerries holds at -12.6%—masking serious underlying issues. Grocery impulse category crashed 40-50% across all stores (Easter panic buying pulled forward to Week 16), while deli/food-to-go surged 16-33%, indicating successful pivots to convenience. Meat margins compressed 2.9-3.6pp despite volume growth, signalling cost inflation or promotional pressure. All three stores face identical external headwinds: Easter YoY calendar shift, post-holiday footfall normalization, and margin erosion in fresh categories.

stores.name: Palmerstown
stores.summary: Palmerstown is underperforming peers, down 18.2% versus -12.6% at Skerries. Scratch bakery collapsed -15.3% (€1,246 loss), deli/FTG strong at +22.6%, but impulse grocery disaster (-54.1%) and meat margin squeeze (-2.9pp) indicate operational vulnerability. Commuter weekday patterns may be offset by Easter holiday absence.

stores.name: Balbriggan
stores.summary: Mid-table performer: -15.0% overall but holding better on fundamentals. Impulse recovery showing (+15.1% latest week vs -40% 4-week), fresh bakery +35.2%, chicken +14.7%. Waste discipline solid at 1.41% total. Margin headwinds consistent (-3.6pp meat) but offset by strong own-brand growth +6.6%.

stores.name: Skerries
stores.summary: Best resilience at -12.6% despite highest base. Produce leader (+15.0%, +€5.4k), fruit +21.2%, chilled fruit +25.0%, organic +43.8%—seasonal strength offsetting impulse collapse. Deli flat (+0.35%), but chicken serveover exceptional +41.8% (+€1.5k). Waste 1.15%, own-brand +8.7%. Tourist/summer footfall may be stabilizing.

drivers.text: Deli & Food-to-Go surged across all stores (+22.6% Palmerstown, +16.4% Balbriggan, +0.35% Skerries), with hot food +33.2%, +21.0%, +25.7% respectively—strong execution on convenience/margin categories.
Skerries produce dominance: +€5.4k weekly (+15.0%), with fruit +21.2% and chilled fruit +25.0%, suggesting seasonal strength and tourist demand stabilization in coastal location.
Grocery impulse category catastrophic: -54.1% Palmerstown, -40.1% Balbriggan, -49.7% Skerries (4-week average). Easter panic buying pulled into Week 16 (pre-holiday), leaving Week 17 depleted.
Meat margin compression: Palmerstown -2.9pp, Balbriggan -3.6pp, despite +1.1% to +9.5% sales growth. Input cost inflation or aggressive promotional discounting eroding profitability.
Bakery volatility: Scratch bakery -15.3% Palmerstown (€1,246 loss) while fresh bakery +35.2% Balbriggan. Inconsistent demand forecast or staffing across stores.
Margin erosion in produce: Skerries fruit margin -3.3pp, chilled fruit -1.5pp despite sales spike—pricing not tracking input cost, or high promotional activity.

priorities: Stabilize meat margins: investigate input costs vs. pricing; audit promotional calendar for week 18-20
Recover impulse grocery baseline: reset merchandising & planograms post-Easter; establish replenishment rhythm
Protect deli/FTG growth: ensure labour scheduling & inventory depth for sustained +20% trajectory
Reconcile bakery forecasts: align Palmerstown scratch output with demand; investigate Balbriggan fresh bakery staffing success
Monitor YoY Easter distortion: segment analysis for Week 16 vs 17 to isolate calendar vs. operational variance

leaks.title: Scratch bakery collapse (Palmerstown only)
leaks.fix: Audit labour scheduling: compare Palmerstown vs Balbriggan scratch staffing hours. Investigate ingredient wastage (current waste data not itemized). Reforecast daily demand by day-of-week; reduce make-to-stock if weekend overstocking evident.

leaks.title: Meat margin squeeze across all stores
leaks.impact: €280-€1,050/week implicit margin loss (2.9-3.6pp on €26-37k sales base)
leaks.fix: Pull supplier price variance reports; compare current cost per kg beef/pork/lamb vs. LY. Identify if promotional discounts (serveovers) are overshooting targets. Re-baseline retail pricing; escalate to Category Manager for margin recovery plan.

leaks.title: Impulse grocery structural decline post-Easter
leaks.fix: Validate: was Week 16 spike genuine +panic demand or forecast error? Pull daily scan data for Week 16 vs 17 to confirm Easter pull-forward. Reset 4-week baseline; implement promotional cycle calendar to smooth demand. Check EPOS for stockout incidents week 16.

leaks.title: Produce margin degradation amid volume surge
leaks.impact: Skerries: -€900/week (1.5-3.3pp on +€5.4k sales); pricing lag vs. cost
leaks.fix: Audit fresh produce supplier invoices; compare landed cost vs. retail pricing week-on-week. Identify if volume spikes (fruit +21%, chilled +25%) accompanied unrecorded markdowns. Adjust pricing matrix for strawberries/berries/citrus for week 18.

leaks.title: Deli prepack discontinuation (Skerries S0092 -100%)
leaks.fix: Investigate: product delisting, supplier issue, or demand pivot to fresh deli? Check inventory records for week 17. If demand-driven, ensure category rebalance to fresh/hot FTG maintains margin. If supplier issue, activate contingency SKU within 48 hours.

urgent.text: Escalate meat margin compression to Category Manager + Supply Chain; request cost variance analysis vs. LY by protein by EOD tomorrow.
Audit Palmerstown scratch bakery: compare daily labour hours, ingredient usage, and daily sales to Balbriggan; identify if overstaffing or underdemand.
Pull detailed EPOS data for impulse grocery Week 16 vs 17; confirm Easter panic-buy pull-forward hypothesis to differentiate calendar vs operational issue.

today: Call all three store managers: confirm Easter week (16) trading reality vs forecast; ask about staffing, customer footfall patterns, and any stock issues.
Request meat supplier pricing reports for week 17; compare cost per unit beef/pork/lamb vs. Week 16 and LY Week 17 to isolate inflation.
Reconcile bakery forecasts: Palmerstown GM to provide daily sales by SKU (fresh vs scratch); Balbriggan to share staffing model for fresh bakery success (35.2% growth).
Validate produce margin decline: pull Skerries fresh produce invoices Week 17; cross-check markup % vs. pricing master; identify any unrecorded discounts.
Schedule weekly (not monthly) impulse grocery replenishment review starting Week 18; establish daily flash reports for bread, milk, tinned goods to prevent stockouts.
Confirm Skerries deli prepack (S0092) status: delisted, out-of-stock, or seasonal? If demand-driven, replan assortment within fresh deli by week 18.

compliance.text: Easter trading period YoY comparison distorted by calendar shift (Easter 2025 vs 2026). Segment Week 16-17 analysis separately; disclose calendar variance in week 18 board report.
Meat waste data incomplete: Palmerstown 2.44%, Balbriggan 3.29%, Skerries not itemized. Audit shrinkage variance; ensure compliance with SuperValu waste tracking SOP by end of week 18.

cross.title: Deli surge masking grocery impulse collapse
cross.why: Food-to-Go +33% (Palmerstown) vs impulse -54% suggests customer basket shift to convenience/prepared foods post-Easter. Margin profile inverse: deli 42-46% vs impulse 21%. Risk: if impulse baseline lost, total profit flat despite sales shift.
cross.action: Quantify YoY profit impact of category mix shift (deli +€ vs impulse -€). If net positive, reinforce deli labour + merchandising; if neutral/negative, implement impulse recovery campaign (price/promotion) for week 18-19.

cross.title: Seasonal produce strength at Skerries offsetting system-wide impulse weakness
cross.why: Skerries holding sales (-12.6%) because produce +15.0% (+€5.4k) absorbs impulse loss. Coastal/tourist demographic and May seasonality (berries, fresh veg) are structural advantages. Palmerstown/Balbriggan (urban commuter/family) lack this buffer.
cross.action: Protect Skerries produce range depth; prioritize organic (+43.8%), fruit (+21.2%), chilled fruit (+25.0%) in allocation. For Palmerstown/Balbriggan, backfill impulse via promotional campaign (soft drinks, snacks, water) week 18-19.

cross.title: Own-brand performance divergence signals pricing/availability opportunity
cross.why: Balbriggan own-brand +6.6% vs Palmerstown -0.3%; Skerries +8.7%. Suggests own-brand is growing in communities where customers are trading up (Balbriggan, Skerries coastal/family) but stagnant in Palmerstown (urban, brand-sensitive). Margin: own-brand 19-22% vs branded 15-18%.
cross.action: Audit Palmerstown own-brand planogram and shelf visibility; run A/B promotion (own-brand vs branded) in week 18. Target: grow Palmerstown own-brand to +5% by end of May.

cross.title: Hourly data anomaly: identical pattern across all three stores
cross.why: Hourly sales/transaction data is character-for-character identical across Palmerstown, Balbriggan, Skerries—impossible given store sizes and demographics. System reporting error.
cross.action: Flag to Pyramid KPI support: request corrected hourly data. Do not make scheduling decisions based on this week's hourly report. Revert to store-specific labour scheduling from previous week until data validated.

diagnosis.wrong: Week 17 sales collapse (-12 to -18%) appears severe, but is primarily a calendar/Easter timing distortion compounded by post-holiday demand normalization, not operational failure.
diagnosis.why: Easter 2025 fell in Week 16 (April 20); Easter 2026 fell in Week 15 (April 19). Week 17 2025 captured post-Easter recovery baseline demand; Week 17 2026 is now depleted post-holiday period. Impulse grocery -40 to -54% is the smoking gun: panic buying (bread, milk, tinned goods) was pulled into Week 15-16 for Easter holidays, leaving Week 17 trough. This is temporary, not structural. However, meat margin compression (-2.9 to -3.6pp) and bakery volatility (Palmerstown -15.3% scratch) are genuine operational issues masked by the calendar noise.
diagnosis.fix: Step 1: Segment analysis—isolate Week 15-16 (Easter impact weeks) from Week 17 onwards. Recalculate 4-week average excluding Week 15. Step 2: Confirm impulse baseline: establish expected demand for bread, milk, bottled water, tinned goods for non-holiday weeks; use Week 18-19 as reset baseline. Step 3: Meat margin recovery—request supplier cost variance by EOD tomorrow; audit promotional depth vs. target. Step 4: Bakery forecast alignment—Palmerstown GM to implement daily forecast-to-actual review; replicate Balbriggan fresh bakery model. Step 5: Monitor Week 18-19 impulse recovery; if still -20% or worse, escalate to Category Management. Target: normalize to -5% or better by end May (accounting for seasonal spring demand patterns).

waste.summary: The Moriarty Group is experiencing significant waste challenges across its three stores, with Balbriggan leading at €1,244.62 in total waste and Skerries at €614.66, while Palmerstown data is unavailable. Critical issues centre on confectionery overstock (Maltesers, Twix, M&Ms generating €481.79 combined in Balbriggan alone with minimal retail sales), perishable produce waste, and dairy products with poor velocity. The total quantifiable waste across Balbriggan and Skerries exceeds €1,859.28, representing a severe inventory management and demand forecasting problem requiring immediate intervention.

stores.name: Palmerstown
stores.action: Obtain waste data immediately to complete group analysis and identify store-specific issues.

stores.name: Balbriggan

top_offenders.insight: Critical ordering failure: 161 units wasted vs only 20 sold represents 8:1 waste-to-sales ratio. Zero markdown support indicates demand forecast error. Reduce order quantities by 50% and implement stricter stock rotation protocols immediately.

Severe overstock: 47 units wasted against 2 retail sales (23.5:1 ratio). This specialty variant is not resonating with customers. Remove from orders and return unsold stock to supplier; revert to standard Twix variants only.

Poor assortment decision: 38 units wasted vs 6 sold (6.3:1 ratio). Specialty flavours lack customer demand at this location. Consolidate confectionery SKUs to proven bestsellers and eliminate low-performing variants.

Produce waste concentration: 17 units wasted vs 8 sold indicates perishability before purchase. Reduce order frequency to 2-3x weekly instead of daily; tighten shelf life management and implement FIFO discipline.

Secondary confectionery issue: Despite reasonable retail sales of 17 units, 32 units wasted suggests display placement or expiry dating problems. Audit bin dates daily and reposition to high-traffic areas.

stores.category_breakdown: Confectionery/Snacks dominating waste (€481.79 from Maltesers, Twix variants, M&Ms, Malt Teasers, Smiths Scampi Fries) driven by specialty/premium variant overstock with weak consumer uptake. Produce category secondary concern (Asparagus, Baby Leaf Salad, Carrot-Cauliflower mix, Broccoli) contributing €100.20 due to short shelf life and ordering excess. Dairy (Yoghurt, Benecol, Greek Yogurt) adding €115.17 from overestimated demand. Pet products anomaly (Pedigree Multivitamins €100.41) suggests categorization or ordering error.
stores.action: Implement immediate confectionery assortment reset: eliminate all specialty chocolate variants with waste:retail ratios >5:1, reduce SKU count by 30%, and establish maximum order caps per SKU based on 12-week rolling average sales. Conduct full produce ordering review to match Balbriggan's demand patterns (smaller, more frequent orders vs bulk purchasing).

stores.name: Skerries

top_offenders.insight: Ordering error with zero retail velocity: 29 units wasted, no sales recorded. Likely supplier delivery error or customer preference mismatch. Discontinue this SKU at Skerries; verify if loose broccoli (sold separately) cannibalizing demand.

Severe dairy waste: 28 units wasted vs 3 sold (9.3:1 ratio). Specialty cream product misaligned with store customer base. Remove from permanent range and order only to customer pre-order/special orders basis.

Complete stock failure: 15 units wasted with zero sales. This appears to be a non-food or non-standard grocery item (potentially a data error). Clarify categorization and remove from food ordering system.
Bakery assortment mismatch: 7 units wasted vs 1 sold indicates this product format/brand underperforms. Test alternative bakery SKUs (individual cakes vs bulk packs) to improve customer alignment.

Zero-sales bakery product: 9 units completely wasted. Remove this specific variant and assess whether Mr Kipling range should be restructured at Skerries (possible cannibalization or customer demographic mismatch).

stores.category_breakdown: Produce waste moderate (Broccoli Crown €29.01, Bananas €25.01, Basil €17.49, Courgette €4.50 = €75.99 total) but efficient relative to retail sales volumes, particularly for Bananas (12 units wasted against 758.86 sold). Dairy category problematic (Spray Cream €92.12, Gravy €50.00, Skim Milk €8.05 = €150.17) driven by specialty/premium items. Bakery significant concern (Mr Kipling variants €62.43 combined) with zero to minimal sales, suggesting poor product-market fit. Deli meats performing better with reduction-supported sales (Brady Family Ham variants €84.50 waste but 93 retail sales combined).
stores.action: Immediately discontinue Broccoli Crown, Spray Cream, and both Mr Kipling variants at Skerries. Audit bakery supplier agreements for flexibility to reduce order minimums. Redirect Mr Kipling space to locally-sourced bakery or proven national brands. For produce, maintain current ordering discipline given strong Banana velocity (0.02 waste ratio) as model for other categories.

group_actions: Establish group-wide SKU waste governance: flag any product with waste-to-retail-sales ratio exceeding 3:1 for immediate delisting or order reduction. Implement weekly waste monitoring dashboard across all three stores with automated alerts at €50+ per SKU weekly waste.

Conduct emergency confectionery range rationalization: audit all specialty chocolate/candy variants across Balbriggan and Skerries; consolidate to core bestsellers only (standard Twix, Mars, Snickers). Eliminate multi-pack variants and premium offerings unless pre-ordered. Estimated waste reduction: €200-300 monthly.

Restructure perishables ordering model: shift from bulk/daily standing orders to demand-driven 2-3x weekly ordering for produce and dairy. Implement 72-hour supplier flexibility agreements and establish maximum bin dates at 50% shelf life to support proactive markdown before waste escalation. Focus on Palmerstown data collection to extend model across all stores.
